---
title: "Unit 7 In Class Discussion"
author:
  - Elizabeth King
  - Kevin Middleton
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    standalone: true
    self-contained: true
    logo: QMLS_Logo.png
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
code-annotations: hover
bibliography: QMLS_Bibliography.bib
csl: evolution.csl
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(tidyverse)
library(cowplot)

theme_set(theme_cowplot(font_size = 20))

```


## Practicing

> I would like more practice with graphing, I’ve encountered different issues each problem set. Also, is it good practice to perform diagnostic checks with every dataset?


## What are confidence intervals really?

> I would appreciate going over quiz 1 - I got a little tripped up on some of the questions

> I wanted to ask about confidence intervals and the null hypothesis (based on Q4 on 07-1 Quiz); if this was using Bayesian statistics and we have some a priori data, then we could discuss the probability of the true mean value, as opposed to just the confidence that we have in that value being the true mean value (frequentist)?



## Questions confidence intervals can't answer

- The *probability* that the true mean is greater than 0 is at least 95%.
- The *probability* that the true mean equals 0 is smaller than 5%
- There is a 95% *probability* that the true mean lies between 0.1 and 0.4.
- The "null *hypothesis*" that the true mean equals 0 is likely to be incorrect.


## Question confidence intervals can answer

- If we were to *repeat* the *experiment* over and over, then in 95% of the trials, the confidence intervals *will contain the true mean*.


## Calculating confidence intervals

Quiz 7-3


## Mysterious Degrees of Freedom

> What exactly is a degree of freedom? How does it affect our outcomes, and is it always n-1? Currently, I am just plugging in this number, but I don't understand what it represents.

> If you can describe what the degree of freedom really means, it would be helpful.


## Relationship between CI and hypothesis tests

> If a hypothesized value falls outside a 95% confidence interval, does that always mean we would reject the null hypothesis?


## Understanding decision criteria

> I know you are giving us examples of how the theory behind the test works, so is the code on slide 10 of lecture 7.3 something we would use to try to see if our mean is something generated by sampling error/chance or would you just calculate a p-value from the t-test to figure that out?


## One-sided vs. two-sided tests

> I'm still a little confused as to when we should choose a One Sided Test versus a Two Sided Test.


## What is Nuzzo talking about?

> When reading through the P value article following lecture 7-3, the authors said, "According to one widely used calculation to an external site., a P value of 0.01 corresponds to a false-alarm probability of at least 11%, depending on the underlying probability that there is a true effect; a P value of 0.05 raises that chance to at least 29%." What calculation is this? Can we talk about this more in depth because this went right over my head.


## What is Nuzzo talking about?

![](https://media.springernature.com/lw703/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2F506150a/MediaObjects/41586_2014_BF506150a_Figb_HTML.jpg)


## Unexpected research questions

> If something initially not part of an experiment becomes of interest through observations during data collection, is there a non biased way to begin collecting and working with data on that trait? Also what can be done to make data more replicable?


## MCMC

> Can you go over the MCMC sampling/ method in a little more detail?


## Frameworks

> Until now, I always thought the frequentist model made just as much sense as the Bayesian model. However, confidence intervals have me rethinking that. Is there an advantage of thinking of true parameters as probabilistic (under Bayesian conception)? Because frequentist CIs, to me, feel like: there is a true mean: you capture it 95% of the time with the CI. But Bayesian feels like: there is a 95% probability this CI captures the mean. Is that correct?

> I don't fully understand Bayesian credible intervals and MCMC, and I am also confused about the difference between Bayesian credible intervals and CI.


## Planning `for` loops

> I have a question about for loops-- what size data set is it best to use these? I have seen examples now with small sets and iterations through a column, but could this get convoluted? It seems you have anticipate what you'll be doing with your data set, but what might indicate a for loop should be used in advance?


## Reviewing `for` loops
